The Register reported that readers of Gizmodo were briefly shown malicious ClickFix-style prompts after an account used to manage parts of the site was compromised. The prompts were designed to persuade users to run commands or take steps that could install malware on their systems.

According to the article, the incident illustrates a growing tactic in which attackers rely on social engineering rather than exploiting a traditional browser vulnerability. In ClickFix schemes, users are typically
articles.content:
The Register reported that readers of Gizmodo were briefly shown malicious ClickFix-style prompts after an account used to manage parts of the site was compromised. The prompts were designed to persuade users to run commands or take steps that could install malware on their systems.

According to the article, the incident illustrates a growing tactic in which attackers rely on social engineering rather than exploiting a traditional browser vulnerability. In ClickFix schemes, users are typically told to paste commands into a system tool under the false claim that the step is needed to fix or verify something.

The report said the compromised content was removed after the issue was identified. It also noted that media sites and other high-traffic platforms can become effective delivery channels when attackers gain access to publishing or advertising systems.

Security guidance cited in the story emphasized that users should be cautious of unexpected instructions asking them to run terminal, PowerShell, or other system commands from a webpage, even when the page appears to come from a familiar brand.

The Verge reported that SK Hynix has passed Samsung Electronics as South Korea's most valuable company by market capitalization, reflecting investor interest in memory chips used for artificial intelligence systems. The shift comes as demand for high-bandwidth memory has increased alongside spending on AI infrastructure.

According to the report, SK Hynix has been viewed as a major beneficiary of that trend because of its position in advanced memory products used in AI servers. Samsung remains o
articles.content:
The Verge reported that SK Hynix has passed Samsung Electronics as South Korea's most valuable company by market capitalization, reflecting investor interest in memory chips used for artificial intelligence systems. The shift comes as demand for high-bandwidth memory has increased alongside spending on AI infrastructure.

According to the report, SK Hynix has been viewed as a major beneficiary of that trend because of its position in advanced memory products used in AI servers. Samsung remains one of the world's largest technology companies, but investor sentiment has been shaped by differing expectations for how each company will perform in the current chip cycle.

The article said the market-value change highlights the importance of AI-related semiconductor demand in South Korea's equity market. It also reflects how quickly leadership can change when investors reassess growth prospects across major hardware suppliers.

Samsung continues to operate across smartphones, consumer electronics, memory, and foundry services, while SK Hynix remains more concentrated in memory. Future rankings are likely to depend on earnings performance, supply conditions, and the durability of AI-driven chip demand.

articles.deck: The Register reported that concerns about space debris are expanding beyond Earth orbit as governments and private companies plan more missions to the Moon.
articles.summary:
The Register reported that concerns about space debris are expanding beyond Earth orbit as governments and private companies plan more missions to the Moon. The article said the increase in lunar activity is raising questions about how to manage waste, avoid interference with future missions, and protect scientifically important sites.

According to the report, Earth orbit already offers examples of how congestion can build when launches accelerate faster than international rules and cleanup sys
articles.content:
The Register reported that concerns about space debris are expanding beyond Earth orbit as governments and private companies plan more missions to the Moon. The article said the increase in lunar activity is raising questions about how to manage waste, avoid interference with future missions, and protect scientifically important sites.

According to the report, Earth orbit already offers examples of how congestion can build when launches accelerate faster than international rules and cleanup systems. Analysts cited in the article said lunar operations could face similar problems if hardware, discarded equipment, and mission remnants accumulate without clear standards.

The article said existing space law provides only limited guidance on property, responsibility, and environmental protection on the lunar surface. That has led to discussion about whether new agreements or operating norms are needed before activity increases further.

The report framed the issue as a policy and coordination challenge rather than an immediate crisis, with the central question being how countries and companies can expand exploration while limiting long-term damage and operational risk.

articles.headline: Developers Warn AI-Built Apps Can Introduce Security Risks When Moved Online
articles.deck: Security researchers, developers, and startup executives told The Verge that applications built largely through AI coding tools can expose sensitive data if they are deployed wi...
articles.summary:
Security researchers, developers, and startup executives told The Verge that applications built largely through AI coding tools can expose sensitive data if they are deployed without adequate safeguards. The report cited cases involving missing authentication, exposed databases, and other flaws discovered after apps were published online.

Several experts said the risks increase when projects that begin as personal or local tools evolve into hosted services that handle other people's information
articles.content:
Security researchers, developers, and startup executives told The Verge that applications built largely through AI coding tools can expose sensitive data if they are deployed without adequate safeguards. The report cited cases involving missing authentication, exposed databases, and other flaws discovered after apps were published online.

Several experts said the risks increase when projects that begin as personal or local tools evolve into hosted services that handle other people's information, including financial, medical, or internal business data. They said public-facing apps generally require stronger review than prototypes or low-risk personal software.

The Verge reported examples in which developers or colleagues found vulnerabilities before or after launch, and cited outside research describing thousands of publicly accessible AI-built apps with weak or missing protections. Experts quoted in the story said automated security checks can help, but warned that those tools are not a substitute for understanding the specific threat model of an application.

The article said some developers are responding by keeping data local, adding repeated security reviews, and seeking outside experts before expanding to more sensitive use cases. The broader question, according to the report, is how software teams will secure a growing volume of code produced with limited direct human review.

Tom's Hardware reviewed Anycubic's Kobra 4 Combo and described it as an incremental update to the Kobra 3 Combo rather than a major redesign. The review said the machine offers a 260 by 260 by 260 millimeter build volume, a quick-release hardened-steel nozzle, automatic bed leveling, and the ACE Pro 2 multi-material system.

According to the review, the printer was easy to assemble and operated quietly, with improved filament handling compared with the earlier ACE system. The outlet also said th
articles.content:
Tom's Hardware reviewed Anycubic's Kobra 4 Combo and described it as an incremental update to the Kobra 3 Combo rather than a major redesign. The review said the machine offers a 260 by 260 by 260 millimeter build volume, a quick-release hardened-steel nozzle, automatic bed leveling, and the ACE Pro 2 multi-material system.

According to the review, the printer was easy to assemble and operated quietly, with improved filament handling compared with the earlier ACE system. The outlet also said the ACE Pro 2 can dry filament while printing and that the printer produced solid results with PLA, PETG, and TPU during testing.

The review identified several limitations, including slow filament swaps, significant purge waste in multi-color printing, a relatively large footprint, and a less convenient setup for TPU when used through the ACE system. It also noted a software issue that underestimated purge waste in the slicer.

Tom's Hardware said the Kobra 4 Combo is positioned as an entry-level option, but concluded that buyers may still prefer competing models, including Anycubic's Kobra X and rival printers in a similar price range, depending on their priorities.

articles.summary:
Apple may raise prices on some products after Chief Executive Tim Cook said the company is facing higher costs tied to an ongoing memory shortage. In comments reported by The Wall Street Journal and cited by 9to5Mac, Cook said the company had tried to absorb supplier increases but could no longer fully do so.

Apple did not say which products would be affected, how large any increase might be, or when changes could take effect. That uncertainty has led to outside speculation about timing, includ
articles.content:
Apple may raise prices on some products after Chief Executive Tim Cook said the company is facing higher costs tied to an ongoing memory shortage. In comments reported by The Wall Street Journal and cited by 9to5Mac, Cook said the company had tried to absorb supplier increases but could no longer fully do so.

Apple did not say which products would be affected, how large any increase might be, or when changes could take effect. That uncertainty has led to outside speculation about timing, including whether adjustments could arrive before the company's fall product launches.

The 9to5Mac article pointed to several possible reasons for a nearer-term move, including pressure on Apple's margins, the company's next earnings report, and an expected leadership transition later this year. Those points were presented as analysis rather than confirmed company plans.

For now, Apple has confirmed only that price increases may be necessary because of component costs. Further details would likely come through product pricing updates or future company disclosures.

Dbrand has opened preorders for a Companion Cube-themed accessory built for Valve's upcoming Steam Machine, according to a report from The Verge.

The accessory is modeled on the Companion Cube from Valve's Portal series and appears to be aimed at early buyers of the new console.

articles.summary:
Sony has not publicly detailed a broad change to its PC release strategy for single-player games, but KitGuru reported that industry journalist Jason Schreier described an internal explanation given to staff by PlayStation executive Hermen Hulst.

According to the report, Schreier wrote on ResetEra that Hulst said first-party single-player narrative games would remain exclusive to PlayStation because PC releases had been inconsistent, had not generated enough revenue and risked weakening the ali
articles.content:
Sony has not publicly detailed a broad change to its PC release strategy for single-player games, but KitGuru reported that industry journalist Jason Schreier described an internal explanation given to staff by PlayStation executive Hermen Hulst.

According to the report, Schreier wrote on ResetEra that Hulst said first-party single-player narrative games would remain exclusive to PlayStation because PC releases had been inconsistent, had not generated enough revenue and risked weakening the alignment between Sony's intellectual property and its platform. Schreier said he confirmed the remarks with two people who heard them.

KitGuru also cited earlier public comments from PlayStation CEO Hideaki Nishino, who said Sony continues to view simultaneous PS5 and PC releases as standard for live-service games that benefit from broader online audiences.

Sony had not issued a direct public statement matching the reported internal remarks at the time of publication.

An article shared on Hacker News described efforts to persuade drawing tablet manufacturers to work more directly with open-source Linux driver developers, and said those efforts have faced resistance tied in part to project branding.

The author, who reviews tablets on GNU/Linux and reports device specifications to developers working on free and open-source drivers, said he had sought to connect manufacturers with technical maintainers involved in the udev-hid-bpf project. According to the post
articles.content:
An article shared on Hacker News described efforts to persuade drawing tablet manufacturers to work more directly with open-source Linux driver developers, and said those efforts have faced resistance tied in part to project branding.

The author, who reviews tablets on GNU/Linux and reports device specifications to developers working on free and open-source drivers, said he had sought to connect manufacturers with technical maintainers involved in the udev-hid-bpf project. According to the post, a representative linked to Gaomon and Huion ultimately declined to participate.

The author quoted an email saying the company viewed key repositories as closely associated with Wacom and did not want to share device specifications in that setting. The post argued that historical Wacom branding across parts of the Linux tablet software stack may discourage competing hardware makers from contributing directly.

The author said he would continue documenting devices individually and sending technical information to developers, while calling for broader stewardship of the driver infrastructure.

articles.content:
A recent Outlook for Mac update has caused replies to omit the original message text, according to user reports cited by The Register.

The issue affects version 16.110, build 26061317, which was released last week, the report said. Users said that before the update, replies automatically included the earlier message, but after the update that conversation history no longer appeared.

According to the report, a Microsoft forum moderator advised affected users to roll back to an earlier version and disable automatic updates until a fix is available. That workaround may be difficult for users on managed devices administered by employers or schools.

Microsoft had not responded to The Register's request for comment at the time of publication. The bug affects one of the standard functions used to preserve email thread context in replies.

articles.summary:
A developer writing on his personal site highlighted new uses for the browser API `window.showDirectoryPicker()`, which allows users to grant a website access to a folder on their device for reading and writing files.

The post described how the feature can support local-first applications, such as note-taking tools that work directly with a user's own Markdown files rather than storing data in a remote service. The author also described prototypes for browsing photo libraries and arranging imag
articles.content:
A developer writing on his personal site highlighted new uses for the browser API `window.showDirectoryPicker()`, which allows users to grant a website access to a folder on their device for reading and writing files.

The post described how the feature can support local-first applications, such as note-taking tools that work directly with a user's own Markdown files rather than storing data in a remote service. The author also described prototypes for browsing photo libraries and arranging image compositions in a browser-based interface.

According to the post, the examples were created with AI assistance and operated directly on files stored on the local filesystem. The author argued that the API could support more advanced browser-based media tools, including photo and video applications that combine web interfaces with local storage.

The feature reflects a broader shift toward web applications that handle more desktop-style tasks while keeping user files on the device.

articles.summary:
Brazil's National Secretariat for Civil Protection and Defense and the Federal Police are investigating a suspected breach of the country's emergency alert system after an unauthorized message was sent to mobile devices in several regions.

According to the report, the platform was taken offline early Saturday, June 20, after users in areas including Sao Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, Parana and the Federal District reported receiving an "extreme" alert containing the word "misantropi4," a variation on
articles.content:
Brazil's National Secretariat for Civil Protection and Defense and the Federal Police are investigating a suspected breach of the country's emergency alert system after an unauthorized message was sent to mobile devices in several regions.

According to the report, the platform was taken offline early Saturday, June 20, after users in areas including Sao Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, Parana and the Federal District reported receiving an "extreme" alert containing the word "misantropi4," a variation on the Portuguese word for misanthropy.

Brazil's telecommunications regulator Anatel said the message was not issued by the authorities responsible for the warning system and said there was no public safety threat related to the alert itself. Civil defense officials said a new dispatch system with stronger security controls was already in development.

articles.headline: Study Finds High Share of AI-Generated Videos on TikTok and YouTube

articles.summary:
A study by video-editing platform Kapwing found that 59% of videos shown to a new TikTok account were AI-generated, while an earlier study found a 21% share on YouTube, according to a report cited by 9to5Mac.

The report said Kapwing analyzed 10,742 TikTok videos across 20 categories and separately reviewed the first 500 videos shown in the For You feed of a newly created account. It also said AI-generated content was especially common in child-focused categories, with 97 of 100 videos in one sa
articles.content:
A study by video-editing platform Kapwing found that 59% of videos shown to a new TikTok account were AI-generated, while an earlier study found a 21% share on YouTube, according to a report cited by 9to5Mac.

The report said Kapwing analyzed 10,742 TikTok videos across 20 categories and separately reviewed the first 500 videos shown in the For You feed of a newly created account. It also said AI-generated content was especially common in child-focused categories, with 97 of 100 videos in one sample tagged #CartoonKids identified as AI-made.

The article said the figures likely undercounted AI-generated content because the study only classified videos with clearly AI-generated scripts and voiceovers. It also noted that recommendation systems may show less of this material over time as users build a viewing history.

Signals intelligence and cybersecurity agencies from Australia, the United States, the United Kingdom, New Zealand and Canada issued a joint warning that advances in frontier AI models could change cyber threats within months rather than years.

According to the statement described by The Guardian, the agencies said AI could improve cyber defense over time while also increasing the speed, scale and sophistication of attacks. They urged organizations to treat cyber risk as a business and leadersh
articles.content:
Signals intelligence and cybersecurity agencies from Australia, the United States, the United Kingdom, New Zealand and Canada issued a joint warning that advances in frontier AI models could change cyber threats within months rather than years.

According to the statement described by The Guardian, the agencies said AI could improve cyber defense over time while also increasing the speed, scale and sophistication of attacks. They urged organizations to treat cyber risk as a business and leadership issue rather than only a technical one.

The report said the warning came after the U.S. government restricted access for foreign nationals to certain Anthropic AI tools. It also cited outside analysis that additional advanced models from other companies or states could emerge soon.

articles.content:

articles.content:

articles.headline: Analysis Examines the 8087 Coprocessor's Shifter Design
articles.deck: A technical analysis of Intel's 8087 floating-point coprocessor examines how the chip's internal shifter was designed to speed up floating-point operations.
articles.summary:
A technical analysis of Intel's 8087 floating-point coprocessor examines how the chip's internal shifter was designed to speed up floating-point operations. The article focuses on the shifter's role in handling tasks such as aligning numbers for arithmetic and supporting operations used in transcendental calculations.

The 8087, introduced in 1980, added floating-point hardware to systems such as the IBM PC. According to the analysis, the chip used a two-stage barrel-shifter design that could sh
articles.content:
A technical analysis of Intel's 8087 floating-point coprocessor examines how the chip's internal shifter was designed to speed up floating-point operations. The article focuses on the shifter's role in handling tasks such as aligning numbers for arithmetic and supporting operations used in transcendental calculations.

The 8087, introduced in 1980, added floating-point hardware to systems such as the IBM PC. According to the analysis, the chip used a two-stage barrel-shifter design that could shift values by 0 to 63 bits in a single step, helping improve performance while keeping the circuit compact enough for the manufacturing limits of the time.

The article also describes the bidirectional nature of the shifter, the control circuitry used to select shift amounts, and the broader significance of the 8087 in the development of floating-point standards later reflected in IEEE 754.

articles.content:
Observable Space, a company developing telescopes, optics and related software, is expanding its role in satellite tracking and optical communications as demand grows for space-based data and monitoring systems.

The company was formed after software venture OurSky combined with telescope manufacturer PlaneWave Instruments, according to Ars Technica. Co-founder and chief executive Dan Roelker previously worked in network security, at DARPA, at Riot Games, at SpaceX and at OpenSea before returning to the space sector.

Observable Space said it aims to provide satellite tracking with high precision and to build hardware for space domain awareness, astronomy and laser-based communications. The company recently received a $94 million US Space Force contract to expand production of optical telescopes and said it is also developing lower-cost optics for spacecraft, including its 200 mm-aperture Iguana telescope.

The article said the company won a contract to build 1,200 telescopes for the Argus Array observatory project and recently raised $90 million in Series A funding. It also described broader industry interest in optical communications, which can transmit more data than conventional radio systems but depend on ground infrastructure and weather conditions.

At maximum fan speed, recorded noise levels ranged from 47dBA for the ASRock Challenger 360 Digital to 55dBA for the DeepCool Spartacus 360. Under steady-state load on the Ryzen 9 9950X, the measured temperature difference b
articles.content:
KitGuru compared seven 360mm all-in-one liquid CPU coolers with integrated displays from ASRock, Cooler Master, CPS PCCOOLER, DeepCool, Enermax and Sudokoo. The review evaluated installation, acoustics, thermal performance and pricing using an AMD Ryzen 9 9950X test system.

At maximum fan speed, recorded noise levels ranged from 47dBA for the ASRock Challenger 360 Digital to 55dBA for the DeepCool Spartacus 360. Under steady-state load on the Ryzen 9 9950X, the measured temperature difference between the best and worst performers was 2 degrees Celsius, with CPS PCCOOLER, DeepCool and Sudokoo posting 59C over ambient and Enermax posting 61C.

At a normalized 40dBA noise target, the ASRock Challenger 360 Digital and ASRock Phantom Gaming 360 LCD both measured 63C over ambient, followed by the Sudokoo Energlo 360 at 64C. In Precision Boost Overdrive testing, most of the coolers produced similar sustained boost behavior, with the leading results clustered around a 52.5x clock multiplier.

The review said performance across the group was closely matched and identified the ASRock Challenger 360 Digital as the strongest value based on its combination of price, acoustics and cooling results.

According to the project materials, Oak is designed around patterns such as one branch per session, branch descriptions instead of individual commit messages, and content-addressed lazy mounts intended to let users begin editing repositories quickly. The project says performance gains for agent
articles.content:
A project highlighted on Hacker News describes Oak as an open-source version-control system built for AI agent workflows, with a reusable library and a command-line client developed as a Cargo workspace.

According to the project materials, Oak is designed around patterns such as one branch per session, branch descriptions instead of individual commit messages, and content-addressed lazy mounts intended to let users begin editing repositories quickly. The project says performance gains for agent workloads follow from that design.

The project includes the oakvcs-core library, which provides content hashing, chunking, diff and merge functions, and a data model for stored objects, along with an optional local repository layer using SQLite and Git backends. It also includes the oakvcs-cli command-line tool.

The materials say the software is in public beta as version 0.99.0, with prebuilt installers for macOS and Linux and other installation options for Windows and Cargo users. The repository is published under the Apache-2.0 license.

articles.headline: Google Outlines Broader Use of AI and Natural-Language Queries in Data Platforms
articles.deck: Google executives said at the Google Cloud Summit in London that the company is expanding the use of AI across its database and analytics products, including features designed t...
articles.summary:
Google executives said at the Google Cloud Summit in London that the company is expanding the use of AI across its database and analytics products, including features designed to support natural-language queries and agent-based workflows.

Sailesh Krishnamurthy, a Google vice president of engineering, said exact SQL queries remain necessary for many workloads, but that customers also want systems that can interpret less precise requests and return useful results. He said those capabilities depen
articles.content:
Google executives said at the Google Cloud Summit in London that the company is expanding the use of AI across its database and analytics products, including features designed to support natural-language queries and agent-based workflows.

Sailesh Krishnamurthy, a Google vice president of engineering, said exact SQL queries remain necessary for many workloads, but that customers also want systems that can interpret less precise requests and return useful results. He said those capabilities depend on infrastructure such as vector indexing, text indexing, graph technology, and enterprise data catalogs that combine structured and unstructured information.

Krishnamurthy said Google is also adding AI functions to products including Spanner, AlloyDB, and BigQuery. He said organizations using AI-generated SQL or similar tools need evaluation sets to test whether generated queries and answers are correct, and to refine prompts and context over time.

Google said one efficiency approach involves smaller proxy models inside the database that can reduce latency and token use compared with full large-language-model calls. Yasmeen Ahmad, a product executive for what Google calls its Agentic Data Cloud, said the company expects users to rely more on systems that execute multi-step tasks from high-level instructions.

The executives said conversational analytics may increasingly supplement traditional dashboards, though the article noted that accuracy, cost, and operational risk remain concerns as AI tools are used more widely in business systems.

articles.summary:
Valve said users can build their own Steam Machine-style PCs with SteamOS beginning with the SteamOS 3.8 release, as the company broadens desktop hardware support, according to The Verge.

SteamOS 3.8.10 launched last week with improved compatibility for recent Intel and AMD platforms. In an interview with The Verge, Valve’s Pierre-Loup Griffais said the company has been working to make the operating system more compatible with desktop hardware and is also collaborating with Nvidia on driver sup
articles.content:
Valve said users can build their own Steam Machine-style PCs with SteamOS beginning with the SteamOS 3.8 release, as the company broadens desktop hardware support, according to The Verge.

SteamOS 3.8.10 launched last week with improved compatibility for recent Intel and AMD platforms. In an interview with The Verge, Valve’s Pierre-Loup Griffais said the company has been working to make the operating system more compatible with desktop hardware and is also collaborating with Nvidia on driver support.

Griffais said Nvidia support may not arrive this year, but described it as ongoing work. The Verge reported that running SteamOS on self-built hardware has previously been possible but has been easier on AMD-based systems than on Intel- or Nvidia-based PCs.

For now, Griffais said the current setup is best suited to console-like PCs connected to a television and using a single hard drive. He said a dedicated SteamOS installer exists, but it is not yet designed to make dual-boot installations alongside another operating system straightforward.

The Verge reported that users can install SteamOS on an existing gaming PC, though doing so requires wiping the drive first.

articles.content:
Valve has opened a reservation system for its Steam Machine, with four configurations available through separate sign-up lists, according to The Verge. The system is designed to allocate early purchase opportunities through a one-time randomization process after the initial sign-up period closes.

The Verge reported that the available models are priced from $1,049 for a 512GB version to $1,428 for a 2TB version bundled with a Steam Controller, with regional pricing variations. The sign-up window runs through June 25 at 10 a.m. Pacific time, after which Valve will determine reservation order for participants.

Users may sign up for multiple configurations, but Valve said each household can ultimately receive only one reservation. If a participant is selected for more than one model, Valve said the reservation will default to the highest-end model chosen.

Valve said it will email participants on June 25 to say whether they received a reservation slot or were placed on a waitlist. Those who receive reservations will have 72 hours to complete their purchase after the first reservation emails begin going out on June 29.

According to The Verge, eligibility requirements include having a Steam account in good standing, having made a Steam purchase before April 27, 2026, and observing a limit of one sign-up per household.

articles.content:
U.S. auto safety regulators have opened a special crash investigation after a Tesla Model 3 struck a home in Texas, killing a 76-year-old woman inside, according to Ars Technica. The Harris County Sheriff’s Office said the driver told investigators an automated driving-assistance system was engaged at the time of the crash.

The sheriff’s office said the driver, Michael Butler, failed to remain in a single lane, left the roadway, and hit the residence at a high rate of speed. Authorities said Butler was not intoxicated and is cooperating with investigators, who are examining whether Tesla’s Autopilot feature played a role.

Ars Technica reported that Martha Avila was in the front room of the home when the vehicle crashed through the structure. Family members told local media that no one else in the house was injured.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration told Ars Technica it is launching a special investigation into the crash. Tesla did not immediately respond to Ars Technica’s request for comment.

The report also noted that Tesla is seeking federal rule changes related to automated vehicles, while safety advocates have argued that some existing requirements should remain in place until the technology is more fully validated.

articles.content:
Google’s DeepMind unit and film studio A24 have entered a multiyear research and development partnership focused on tools for movie production and distribution, according to announcements cited by The Verge and The Wall Street Journal.

The Wall Street Journal reported that Google is investing about $75 million in A24, which would mark the company’s first stake in a film studio. Google said the collaboration is intended to help creators develop new workflows and techniques, though it did not identify specific film projects.

According to The Wall Street Journal, the agreement is non-exclusive and does not give Google access to A24’s film and television library data. The partnership comes as entertainment companies continue to debate the use of artificial intelligence and copyright issues tied to training models on publicly available internet data.

The Verge also reported that the two companies hope to involve artists associated with A24. Scott Belsky, an A24 partner and former Adobe chief strategy officer, told The Wall Street Journal that the tools under development would be aimed at preserving creative control and supporting experimentation.

articles.headline: Ukraine Opens TrophyLab Database for Analysis of Captured Russian Equipment
articles.deck: Ukraine’s Ministry of Defense has launched an online platform called TrophyLab to share data on captured Russian military equipment with approved partners, according to The Regi...
articles.summary:
Ukraine’s Ministry of Defense has launched an online platform called TrophyLab to share data on captured Russian military equipment with approved partners, according to The Register. The ministry said the platform is intended to support analysis of Russian weapons and systems by Ukrainian institutions and allied governments and defense organizations.

The ministry said TrophyLab currently includes material from more than 115 captured items across 79 categories, including missiles, aircraft, dron
articles.content:
Ukraine’s Ministry of Defense has launched an online platform called TrophyLab to share data on captured Russian military equipment with approved partners, according to The Register. The ministry said the platform is intended to support analysis of Russian weapons and systems by Ukrainian institutions and allied governments and defense organizations.

The ministry said TrophyLab currently includes material from more than 115 captured items across 79 categories, including missiles, aircraft, drones, electronic warfare systems, tanks, and small arms. It also said the database contains more than 225 prior studies of seized equipment, with additional material being provided by Ukrainian defense forces, intelligence agencies, and the Security Service of Ukraine.

The Register cited a Center for Strategic and International Studies estimate published in 2025 that Russia lost more than 1,000 armored fighting vehicles, at least 3,000 infantry fighting vehicles, 300 self-propelled artillery units, and nearly 2,000 tanks between January 2024 and June 2025.

Access to TrophyLab is limited. According to The Register, eligible users include Ukrainian scientific organizations, military units, defense-sector firms, allied governments, defense departments, and defense contractors in partner countries. Applicants must also show they have no ties to Russia and meet other conditions set by Ukraine’s defense ministry.

articles.content:

The article said Postgres emerged from Stonebraker's earlier Ingres work and was later revived by volunteer developers who added SQL support and continued development after Stonebraker had largely moved on from the project.

According to the report, Postgres benefited
articles.content:
Postgres has grown from an academic project into one of the world's most widely used database systems, according to The Register, which traced its history through remarks by database pioneer Michael Stonebraker at PGDay in Boston.

The article said Postgres emerged from Stonebraker's earlier Ingres work and was later revived by volunteer developers who added SQL support and continued development after Stonebraker had largely moved on from the project.

According to the report, Postgres benefited from its extensible architecture, permissive licensing and broad compatibility, which helped make it a foundation for cloud database services and derivative systems. The article said major cloud providers now offer Postgres-based services.

It also said the project still lacks some features found in commercial databases, including file-level encryption, and that contributors continue to debate whether those additions fit Postgres's technical priorities and general-purpose design philosophy.

articles.content:
CD Projekt Red co-chief executive Michał Nowakowski said he does not believe the company has fully completed its reputation recovery following the troubled launch of Cyberpunk 2077, according to the report.

According to the article, the bug is in the Boot ROM, the first
articles.content:
A Barcelona-based offensive cybersecurity company has published details and a proof of concept for a vulnerability in Apple chips that could help researchers gain low-level access to older iPhones, according to TechCrunch.

The report said the flaw, called usbliter8, affects devices using Apple's A12 and A13 chips, including models from the iPhone XS and XR through the iPhone 11. Exploitation requires physical access to the device.

According to the article, the bug is in the Boot ROM, the first code that runs when an iPhone starts. Because that code is embedded in the chip, the flaw cannot be fixed through a software update. Paradigm Shift said moving to newer hardware is the most effective mitigation.

TechCrunch said the release could aid further research into jailbreaks or forensic access techniques, though additional vulnerabilities would still be needed to reach user data or fully compromise a device.

The projects were presented at ISC 2026 and include installations at national supercomputing centers, EuroHPC AI factories and research institutions. The article said planned systems include projects at the Barcelona Supercomputing Center, BavariaAI, IT4LIA, HLRS and Linköping University.

According to the report, the systems will use Nvidia hardware and software platforms including Hopper, Blackwell and future Vera Rubin products, along with related networking and AI software tools. It said the projects are intended to support research in fields including climate modeling, health, energy, robotics and public-sector AI services.

The article also highlighted the Jupiter system in Germany and said additional Rubin-based systems are being developed by research institutions in Europe and the United States. Timelines and final capabilities will depend on deployment schedules and production of the underlying hardware.

articles.deck: JEDEC has approved SPHBM4, a new memory standard designed to preserve HBM4-level performance while using fewer signal pins and more conventional packaging methods, according to ...
articles.summary:
JEDEC has approved SPHBM4, a new memory standard designed to preserve HBM4-level performance while using fewer signal pins and more conventional packaging methods, according to the report. The approval followed review by the DRAM Memory Subcommittee and the organization's board.

The article said the standard is intended to reduce reliance on advanced packaging, which has become a significant cost factor as demand for high-bandwidth memory rises in artificial intelligence and high-performance co
articles.content:
JEDEC has approved SPHBM4, a new memory standard designed to preserve HBM4-level performance while using fewer signal pins and more conventional packaging methods, according to the report. The approval followed review by the DRAM Memory Subcommittee and the organization's board.

The article said the standard is intended to reduce reliance on advanced packaging, which has become a significant cost factor as demand for high-bandwidth memory rises in artificial intelligence and high-performance computing systems. It said SPHBM4 seeks to offset a lower pin count by increasing signal speeds by a factor of four while cutting required pins to about one-fifth of HBM4 levels.

According to the report, the design also increases the distance between memory and compute die to 20 millimeters, a change described as beneficial for thermal management.

The article said analysts and industry participants see potential for SPHBM4 to be used with future glass substrates, which are still moving toward commercial production. Wider adoption, if it occurs, could expand the range of systems able to use HBM-class memory.

ESA said observations by the James Webb Space Telescope indicate that interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS has an unusual chemical composition that may point to an origin 10 billion to 12 billion years ago.

According to ESA, the telescope measured high deuterium levels and low carbon-13 levels compared with solar system comets. Researchers said those results suggest the comet formed in a very cold, dense environment early in the galaxy’s history.

ESA said 3I/ATLAS is the third confirmed interstellar co
articles.content:
ESA said observations by the James Webb Space Telescope indicate that interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS has an unusual chemical composition that may point to an origin 10 billion to 12 billion years ago.

According to ESA, the telescope measured high deuterium levels and low carbon-13 levels compared with solar system comets. Researchers said those results suggest the comet formed in a very cold, dense environment early in the galaxy’s history.

ESA said 3I/ATLAS is the third confirmed interstellar comet and was observed as it moved away from the Sun in December 2025. A paper on the findings was published June 22 in Nature.

ESA also cited a separate study using the European Southern Observatory’s Very Large Telescope that analyzed carbon and nitrogen varieties in cyanide to complement the Webb observations.

articles.headline: Webb Study Finds Interstellar Comet 3I/ATLAS Has Unusual Isotopic Composition

articles.summary:
NASA said observations by the James Webb Space Telescope found that interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS contains chemical ratios not seen in solar system comets, offering new clues about where and when it formed.

According to NASA, the telescope’s NIRSpec instrument detected deuterium levels about 30 times higher than those measured in solar system comets, as well as only trace amounts of carbon-13 relative to carbon-12. Researchers said those measurements suggest the comet formed 10 billion to 12 bill
articles.content:
NASA said observations by the James Webb Space Telescope found that interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS contains chemical ratios not seen in solar system comets, offering new clues about where and when it formed.

According to NASA, the telescope’s NIRSpec instrument detected deuterium levels about 30 times higher than those measured in solar system comets, as well as only trace amounts of carbon-13 relative to carbon-12. Researchers said those measurements suggest the comet formed 10 billion to 12 billion years ago in a cold, dense environment early in the Milky Way’s history.

NASA said 3I/ATLAS is the third confirmed interstellar comet observed in the solar system. The agency said the object was studied as it moved away from the Sun in December 2025, when warming had produced a gas coma suitable for measurement.

A related study using the European Southern Observatory’s Very Large Telescope examined carbon and nitrogen in cyanide, complementing the Webb findings, NASA said.

Rocket Lab launched a satellite from New Zealand on Friday as part of the U.S. Space Force’s Victus Haze mission, a military exercise designed to test how quickly the service can respond to events in low Earth orbit.

articles.content:
Rocket Lab launched a satellite from New Zealand on Friday as part of the U.S. Space Force’s Victus Haze mission, a military exercise designed to test how quickly the service can respond to events in low Earth orbit.

According to the report, the newly cataloged satellite, designated Victus Haze Puma, entered a polar orbit after launch from Rocket Lab’s site at Māhia Peninsula. The mission is intended to demonstrate a rapid-response capability in which one satellite approaches another object already in orbit to assess a potential threat.

The report said True Anomaly’s Jackal-0004 satellite, launched in May on a SpaceX rideshare mission, is serving as the other spacecraft in the exercise. Based on open-source tracking data cited in the report, the two satellites came within about 60 miles of each other roughly eight hours after Puma reached orbit.

Victus Haze follows the Space Force’s Victus Nox demonstration in 2023. The report said the program combines government funding and private capital and is intended to support future rapid on-orbit operations.

articles.headline: Study Suggests Interstellar Comet 3I/ATLAS May Predate Solar System by Billions of Years
articles.deck: Researchers said observations of interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS suggest it may be as much as 12 billion years old, which would make it far older than the 4.5-billion-year-old solar...
articles.summary:
Researchers said observations of interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS suggest it may be as much as 12 billion years old, which would make it far older than the 4.5-billion-year-old solar system.

According to a study published in Nature, measurements from the James Webb Space Telescope and the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array found isotopic ratios unlike those seen in solar system comets. Lead author Martin Cordiner of NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center told Agence France-Presse the findings
articles.content:
Researchers said observations of interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS suggest it may be as much as 12 billion years old, which would make it far older than the 4.5-billion-year-old solar system.

According to a study published in Nature, measurements from the James Webb Space Telescope and the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array found isotopic ratios unlike those seen in solar system comets. Lead author Martin Cordiner of NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center told Agence France-Presse the findings could indicate the comet formed in a very cold environment early in the Milky Way’s history, though he said alternative explanations remain possible.

The study found elevated levels of deuterium and relatively low levels of carbon-13, which researchers said point to an ancient origin. Scientists said the comet may have spent billions of years traveling through interstellar space after being ejected from its original planetary system.

3I/ATLAS is the third confirmed interstellar object observed passing through the solar system. Researchers said future observations may become more difficult as the comet leaves the solar system.

articles.content:
NASA said astronauts Chris Williams and Jessica Meir are scheduled to conduct a spacewalk from the International Space Station on Tuesday, June 30, to replace a wrist joint on the Canadarm2 robotic arm.

The agency said the spacewalk is expected to begin at about 8:35 a.m. EDT and last about six and a half hours. NASA and the Canadian Space Agency plan to hold a preview news conference on Thursday, June 25, at Johnson Space Center in Houston.

According to NASA, the joint malfunctioned on May 27 when the arm drew elevated motor current and did not move as expected. The agency said a spare part already aboard the station will be used for the repair.

NASA said the outing will be the second spacewalk for Williams, the fifth for Meir, and the 280th conducted in support of station assembly, maintenance, and upgrades.

articles.content:
Commercial space surveillance company LeoLabs said it detected an unidentified object near China’s Shenlong reusable spaceplane on June 22 and assessed with high confidence that the object was released from the spacecraft during its fourth mission.

According to the report, China launched the spaceplane aboard a Long March 2F rocket from Jiuquan Satellite Launch Center on February 7, and the vehicle later raised its orbit to about 593 kilometers. LeoLabs said the object was first observed by its Kiwi Space Radar in New Zealand and did not match another object in its catalog.

The report said the object had not yet appeared in the U.S. Space Force’s Space-Track catalog at the time of publication. It also said earlier Shenlong missions appeared to include release of subsatellites and rendezvous and proximity operations.

China has disclosed few details about the program, describing it through Xinhua as an experimental reusable spacecraft project for peaceful space use. Analysts cited in the report said publicly available imagery suggests the vehicle may be comparable in size and function to the U.S. X-37B.

articles.content:
NASA said its Experimental Fabrication Branch at Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, builds, modifies, and repairs hardware for the center’s research and science aircraft.

According to NASA, the branch provides services including precision machining, sheet-metal forming, aircraft tubing, welding, additive manufacturing, composite fabrication, and structural repair. The agency said the team works on custom hardware for aircraft and ground-based research applications.

NASA said the branch supported development of the AIRVUE sensor pod for autonomous-flight research by turning digital designs into a flight-ready structure that met safety requirements. The agency also said the group assists with local robotics competitions by repairing student-built robots and demonstrating fabrication techniques.

NASA said the branch uses computer-aided design and manufacturing tools including Pro E/Creo, MasterCam, and SolidWorks, and works with engineering teams from early design through final assembly and aircraft installation.

articles.content:
Heavy investment by Gulf states in club soccer has not yet translated into strong World Cup results for their national teams, according to interviews and match results reviewed by Politico.

The United Arab Emirates did not qualify for the tournament, Qatar suffered a lopsided loss, and Saudi Arabia needed a win in its next match to remain in contention after a defeat to Spain. Saudi team manager Georgios Donis said foreign stars in the domestic league may improve competition over time, but said the national team also needs experience and mentality to compete at the highest level.

People familiar with Saudi soccer strategy told Politico that officials are trying to avoid the model used in China, where expensive foreign signings brought attention but did not significantly improve national-team performance. Officials and industry figures said Saudi Arabia views the project as long term, with added pressure to show progress before it hosts the men's World Cup in 2034.

articles.content:
Jordan's first appearance at a men's World Cup is giving the country an opportunity to present itself to U.S. audiences as more than a security-focused story, Ambassador Dina Kawar said in an interview with Politico.

Kawar said the tournament could strengthen national pride, attract sponsorships and support further investment in Jordan's sports infrastructure. Kawar said Jordan planned fan events in Dallas and Santa Clara to promote tourism and business, highlighting destinations including Petra, Wadi Rum and the Dead Sea. She described the World Cup as the start of a new phase for Jordanian soccer and a broader opening for cultural visibility.

articles.headline: Report says fathers want more caregiving roles but face economic and policy barriers

The report, "State of the World's Fathers 2026," draws on interviews with 5,000 fathers in countries including Brazil, Canada and Croatia. Researchers said fathers reported being more involved in childcare than their own fathers were, but still less invol
articles.content:
A new report from Equimundo says many fathers want to play a larger role in raising children, but economic pressure, limited paid leave and persistent gender norms continue to push many families toward more traditional caregiving arrangements.

The report, "State of the World's Fathers 2026," draws on interviews with 5,000 fathers in countries including Brazil, Canada and Croatia. Researchers said fathers reported being more involved in childcare than their own fathers were, but still less involved than mothers. The report cites limited paternity leave, wage disparities between men and women, and lack of awareness of parenting resources aimed at fathers as barriers to more equal parenting.

The article also points to high levels of parental stress, especially in the United States, where paid parental leave remains limited and childcare costs are high. According to the report, nearly a quarter of surveyed fathers worldwide reported poor well-being, while many said they took overtime or additional jobs to cover childcare costs.

Am Johal, a former leader of the city's anti-Olympics movement, said governments are projected to spend more than $1 billion to host World Cup matches in Vancouver and Toronto, and argued that officials should weigh the opportunity cost of that public fun
articles.content:
Vancouver's role as a World Cup host city has drawn less visible protest than the resistance that surrounded the 2010 Winter Olympics, even as critics continue to question public spending and policing tied to large international sports events.

Am Johal, a former leader of the city's anti-Olympics movement, said governments are projected to spend more than $1 billion to host World Cup matches in Vancouver and Toronto, and argued that officials should weigh the opportunity cost of that public funding. More than 70 percent of voters in both cities told Angus Reid polling that hosting was not worth the cost, according to Politico.

The article notes that earlier opposition to the Olympics united housing activists, civil-liberties advocates, environmental groups and anti-poverty organizers. This year, legal observers and activists have monitored new FIFA-related bylaws and police surveillance tools, but the city has not seen the same level of street protest. British Columbia Premier David Eby, once associated with civil-liberties criticism of the Olympics, has publicly welcomed the event as host-city preparations continue.

articles.summary:

The analysis reported an 84.4 percent increase in speeding deaths after dark in Florida during that period. It said the state averaged 206.2 nighttime speeding fatalities a year, compared with 111.8 during daylight hours, with 64.8
articles.content:
A study by Florida-based Blakely Law Firm found that Florida ranked second among U.S. states for growth in nighttime speeding fatalities between 2020 and 2024, based on data from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration's Fatality Analysis Reporting System.

The analysis reported an 84.4 percent increase in speeding deaths after dark in Florida during that period. It said the state averaged 206.2 nighttime speeding fatalities a year, compared with 111.8 during daylight hours, with 64.8 percent of speeding deaths occurring at night. Rhode Island ranked first in percentage increase, though its total number of deaths was much lower.

The study also placed Louisiana in the top 10 for increases in nighttime speeding deaths. Alaska, Wyoming and Vermont were listed among the states with the lowest levels in the report.

articles.content:
Iran's national soccer team remained on the field after a scoreless draw with Belgium at SoFi Stadium on Sunday, applauding supporters as fans in the crowd displayed both official national colors and the pre-revolution lion-and-sun flag.

The flag is associated with opposition to Iran's current government and is banned by FIFA, creating a visible contrast between the team representing the Islamic Republic and some of the supporters cheering it. One fan from Seattle said he had traveled to the match to support the team despite the surrounding political controversy.

Iran's final group-stage match is scheduled for June 26 in Seattle against Egypt. The game will take place during the city's Pride celebrations, a combination that has prompted formal complaints from both countries, according to Politico.

articles.headline: U.S.-Iran talks begin in Switzerland as disputes persist over shipping, sanctions and regional fighting
articles.summary:
U.S. and Iranian officials opened talks in Switzerland on Sunday over next steps following an interim agreement reached last week, even as disagreements continued over regional conflict, shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and Iran's nuclear program.

The U.S. delegation is led by Vice President JD Vance and includes Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff, while Iran's team is led by Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf and Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi. Pakistan and Qatar are serving as mediators
articles.content:
U.S. and Iranian officials opened talks in Switzerland on Sunday over next steps following an interim agreement reached last week, even as disagreements continued over regional conflict, shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and Iran's nuclear program.

The U.S. delegation is led by Vice President JD Vance and includes Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff, while Iran's team is led by Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf and Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi. Pakistan and Qatar are serving as mediators. Iran said the talks must address Israel's military campaign in Lebanon, while U.S. officials said commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz was continuing despite Tehran's statement that it had again closed the waterway.

President Donald Trump said the United States could impose tolls in the strait if no final agreement is reached within 60 days. Other issues under discussion include access to frozen Iranian assets and Iran's nuclear program. An official familiar with the talks told The Associated Press that the Iranian delegation remained engaged and had not indicated to mediators that it planned to leave.

articles.summary:
France Sports Minister Marina Ferrari said in New York that French broadcasters had agreed not to run advertising during World Cup hydration breaks, while acknowledging that extreme weather and climate pressures may require such pauses in future tournaments.

In an interview after an event at the French consulate, Ferrari said French national team players are free as citizens to express political views, but should separate those views from their role while representing France on the field. She a
articles.content:
France Sports Minister Marina Ferrari said in New York that French broadcasters had agreed not to run advertising during World Cup hydration breaks, while acknowledging that extreme weather and climate pressures may require such pauses in future tournaments.

In an interview after an event at the French consulate, Ferrari said French national team players are free as citizens to express political views, but should separate those views from their role while representing France on the field. She also said climate change is making snow conditions less certain for future Winter Olympics and called for a more restrained model for hosting those games.

Asked about Qatar-backed ownership of Paris Saint-Germain, Ferrari said France was fortunate to have the club and declined to criticize its investors.

A guest commentary in Florida Politics said Florida employers continue to face shortages of middle- and high-skill workers as the state's economy grows. The article argued for closer coordination between schools, colleges, employers and workforce agencies to meet demand in industries including logistics, manufacturing, aerospace, information technology and life sciences.

The piece said apprenticeships, internships and industry-recognized certifications are important tools for narrowing skills g
articles.content:
A guest commentary in Florida Politics said Florida employers continue to face shortages of middle- and high-skill workers as the state's economy grows. The article argued for closer coordination between schools, colleges, employers and workforce agencies to meet demand in industries including logistics, manufacturing, aerospace, information technology and life sciences.

The piece said apprenticeships, internships and industry-recognized certifications are important tools for narrowing skills gaps and described workforce development as a core part of economic infrastructure.

The commentary also cited the Florida Chamber Foundation's Florida 2030 Blueprint and said that for every 100 open jobs, 116 Floridians are looking for work.

The article said these issues would be discussed at the Florida Learners to Earners Workforce Solution Summit on June 23.

Wilson is running against former Clearwater City Council Member Mark Bunker and Kevin Laughlin, who chairs the Clearwater Marine Advisory Board and operates a local business. According to the report, Wilson had raised nearly $22,000 by the end of May and had about $18,000 on hand. Bunker had raised less than $5,000 and Laughlin about $2,000.

The election is scheduled for Aug. 18. If no candidate wins more than 50% of the vote, the top two finishers will advance to a Nov. 3 runoff under a charter amendment approved in 2024.

Vice President JD Vance said Monday that talks with senior Iranian officials in Switzerland created what he described as a foundation for a possible final agreement, according to an Associated Press report carried by PBS. The negotiations are aimed at reaching a permanent end to the war involving the United States, Israel and Iran.

Mediators said the discussions, which began Sunday and continued into early Monday, produced some agreements while technical talks continue this week. Iran said ther
articles.content:
Vice President JD Vance said Monday that talks with senior Iranian officials in Switzerland created what he described as a foundation for a possible final agreement, according to an Associated Press report carried by PBS. The negotiations are aimed at reaching a permanent end to the war involving the United States, Israel and Iran.

Mediators said the discussions, which began Sunday and continued into early Monday, produced some agreements while technical talks continue this week. Iran said there had been progress on efforts to halt fighting in Lebanon, which its officials described as an early test of the negotiations.

The U.S. Treasury also issued a 60-day license waiving sanctions on Iranian oil under an interim agreement, authorizing production, delivery and sale through Aug. 21, the report said.

Vance said additional talks will focus on issues including Iran's nuclear program, the Strait of Hormuz and maintaining a ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah in southern Lebanon.

President Donald Trump had said he wanted the pool renovated in time for summer events in the capital. The article examined Trump's claim that vandalism affected the project and described the broader issues su
articles.content:
A $14 million project to renovate the Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool in Washington has faced delays as the country approaches its 250th anniversary celebrations, according to The Guardian. The report said the work has encountered problems including algae, peeling paint and rising costs.

President Donald Trump had said he wanted the pool renovated in time for summer events in the capital. The article examined Trump's claim that vandalism affected the project and described the broader issues surrounding the renovation effort.

The report said the project has drawn public attention because of the site's visibility and the administration's deadline for completing the work.

articles.content:
Florida House Democratic Leader Fentrice Driskell has received endorsements from the Florida AFL-CIO, the West Central Florida Labor Council, Tampa Firefighters Local 754, the Suncoast Police Benevolent Association, Teamsters Local Union 79 and the Suncoast Sierra Club in the Democratic primary for Senate District 16. The seat is open because Sen. Darryl Rouson is leaving office due to term limits.

Driskell is running against fellow Democrat Michele Rayner in an August primary that will be open to all voters because no Republican entered the race. According to the report, registered Democrats make up 47% of voters in the district and Republicans 23%.

The article said Driskell has raised nearly $190,000 for her campaign and has more than $335,000 in two affiliated political committees. Rayner has raised nearly $87,000 and has about $42,000 in an affiliated political committee.

The report also cited polling that found Rayner leading Driskell 26% to 22% in mid-May, with 41% of voters undecided, after an earlier April poll of Democrats alone showed a wider Rayner lead.

The article says AI-focused super PACs have raised more than $100 million during the 2026 election cycle and spent about $49 million so far, with roughly half of that spending directed to the Manhattan-based NY-12 race. Much of the spending has targeted state Assemblymember Alex Bores, who sponsored New Yor
articles.content:
A Democratic primary for New York's 12th Congressional District has become a focal point for political spending tied to artificial intelligence policy, according to a report by The Guardian.

The article says AI-focused super PACs have raised more than $100 million during the 2026 election cycle and spent about $49 million so far, with roughly half of that spending directed to the Manhattan-based NY-12 race. Much of the spending has targeted state Assemblymember Alex Bores, who sponsored New York's Raise Act, described in the article as the second state law requiring major AI developers to publish public safety plans.

The report says Think Big, affiliated with the pro-AI network Leading the Future, has spent $8.2 million in the primary. Federal Election Commission data cited by the article says Leading the Future's $75 million war chest comes from four donors: venture capitalists Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz, and OpenAI co-founder Greg Brockman and his wife, Anna Brockman. Other groups, including Public First and You Can Push Back, have spent heavily in response while arguing for stronger AI safeguards.

The article describes the race as a proxy fight over how AI should be regulated, with debate over federal standards, state laws and the political influence of technology companies. The primary is scheduled for Tuesday.

articles.content:
Former Senate Democratic Leader Audrey Gibson and state Rep. Kim Daniels are leading fundraising in separate Democratic House primaries in Duval County, according to campaign finance figures reported by Florida Politics.

In House District 13, Gibson has raised more than $17,000 and spent just over $2,500. Her opponent, lawyer and activist Leslie Jean-Bart, has raised $2,350, loaned her campaign $2,000 and spent just over $2,000. The district includes parts of west and northwest Jacksonville and is considered strongly Democratic.

In House District 14, Daniels has loaned her campaign $35,000 and raised nearly $22,000 more. Her opponent, Rhian Tutson, has raised more than $1,800 and loaned herself $150. The district includes the Northside, Downtown, Brooklyn, Arlington and parts of the Southside.

The article said no one filed to close the primaries, meaning all voters in the districts will be eligible to vote on Aug. 18.

articles.content:
Outgoing state Rep. Lindsay Cross has endorsed Lindsay Polega-Quigley in the race to succeed her in Florida House District 60.

Cross, a Democrat from St. Petersburg, recently announced she would not seek re-election and urged supporters to back Polega-Quigley. The article says Polega-Quigley entered the race hours after that announcement and is campaigning on affordability, reproductive health care, voting rights, water quality and flooding.

Republican Melissa Rutland is also running and has received endorsements from Pinellas County Tax Collector Adam Ross and several county commissioners, according to the report. The district is considered competitive, and Cross won re-election in 2024 with 54% of the vote.

Neither candidate faces a primary opponent, setting up a head-to-head general election contest. Polega-Quigley's campaign kickoff is scheduled for June 29 in downtown St. Petersburg.

articles.content:
The Seminole Education Association has endorsed incumbents Autumn Garick and Kristine Kraus, along with newcomer Holton Mills, in the Aug. 18 Seminole County School Board election.

Kraus represents District 1 and Garick represents District 5. In District 2, Mills is running for an open seat against School Resource Officer Joshua Memminger because current board member Kelley Davis is not on the ballot.

SEA President Thomas Bugos said the three endorsed candidates understand school system challenges and would work with educators. The article said the union's announcement included statements from all three candidates.

Garick is a former literacy educator. Mills chairs the Foundation for Seminole County Public Schools and works as an account executive at evok advertising. Kraus is identified in the article as a former preschool teacher and parent of four Seminole County students.

articles.content:
Former Senate Democratic Leader Lauren Book has received endorsements from the Florida Professional Firefighters and Metro-Broward Professional Fire Fighters Local 3080 in her campaign for Senate District 30.

The article says the endorsements add labor support from organizations representing firefighters, paramedics, emergency medical technicians and other emergency personnel in Broward and Palm Beach counties. Union leaders cited Book's past support for first-responder issues, including a 2018 law that expanded workers' compensation benefits for first responders diagnosed with post-traumatic stress disorder without an accompanying physical injury.

Book said she would continue working on mental health protections and other support for emergency personnel. The statewide firefighters' organization represents more than 30,000 members, according to the article, while Local 3080 represents more than 800 firefighters and paramedics in Broward County.

The report also said Book raised more than $59,000 in her first 10 days as a candidate and previously received an endorsement from U.S. Rep. Jared Moskowitz. The primary is Aug. 18 and the general election is Nov. 3.

articles.content:
The Broward AFL-CIO has endorsed Democrat Michael Ferguson in the race for Florida House District 100, adding organized labor support to his campaign for the open seat.

The district is being vacated by term-limited Republican Rep. Chip LaMarca and is viewed by Democrats as a pickup opportunity. In a statement reported by Florida Politics, Broward AFL-CIO President Andy Madtes said Ferguson would support jobs, workplace safety and organizing rights.

The article said Ferguson has raised more than $100,000 and knocked on more than 4,000 doors since entering the race. It also noted earlier support from VoteVets. Ferguson is an Air Force veteran and attorney at Krupnick Campbell.

House District 100 runs along the Broward County coast from Fort Lauderdale to Deerfield Beach. The primary is Aug. 18, followed by the general election on Nov. 3.

The article, written by Steve Cona of ABC Florida Gulf Coast, says nearly 50,000 people work in construction in Hillsborough County and that member companies of the trade group employ about 32,500 people across the Tampa Bay region. It also says more than 20,000 registered apprentices in Florida are tra
articles.content:
An opinion article in Florida Politics argues that major public construction projects can have broader economic effects through job creation, apprenticeship training and local business activity.

The article, written by Steve Cona of ABC Florida Gulf Coast, says nearly 50,000 people work in construction in Hillsborough County and that member companies of the trade group employ about 32,500 people across the Tampa Bay region. It also says more than 20,000 registered apprentices in Florida are training in construction-related trades while earning wages.

Cona cites industry economic models that estimate every $100 million invested in commercial construction supports nearly 900 jobs and generates about $165 million in economic output. He argues that debate over the proposed Tampa Bay Rays stadium should include consideration of those workforce and economic effects.

The article reflects the author's view in favor of construction investment and identifies him as president and chief executive of ABC Florida Gulf Coast and a former Hillsborough County School Board member.

It also applies a wage threshold of $22.53 an hour, or $46,860 a year, to focus on highe
articles.content:
An opinion article published by Florida Politics highlights a new Florida Chamber Foundation website that connects projected high-demand jobs in the state with math proficiency requirements.

The article says the interactive site lists 30 occupations with strong projected growth and replacement demand through 2033 and allows users to explore data by workforce region, education level and specific math skills. It also applies a wage threshold of $22.53 an hour, or $46,860 a year, to focus on higher-paying jobs.

Author Chad Aldeman says the tool supports broader policy discussions about aligning education with labor market demand. He writes that making math requirements visible could help families, educators and policymakers better understand how academic preparation relates to future employment.

The article identifies Aldeman as an education policy adviser to the Collaborative for Student Success.

U.S. District Judge Martin Fitzpatrick ordered Fishback to disclose information related to a Cartier watch valued at about $7,500, a Tesla Model 3 and two bank accounts. The case stems from earlier orders requiring Fishback to turn over company s
articles.content:
A federal judge was expected to consider next steps after gubernatorial candidate James Fishback did not meet a June 18 deadline to provide financial information in a civil dispute with his former employer, Greenlight Capital, according to the article.

U.S. District Judge Martin Fitzpatrick ordered Fishback to disclose information related to a Cartier watch valued at about $7,500, a Tesla Model 3 and two bank accounts. The case stems from earlier orders requiring Fishback to turn over company stock certificates to the U.S. Marshals Service after litigation with Greenlight, where he previously worked as an analyst.

The article said Fishback had not filed the requested information by the deadline and had not recently submitted filings in the dispute. It quoted Fitzpatrick as saying the court could grant a motion by default if a required memorandum was not filed.

Fishback has continued campaigning for governor during the case. Florida Politics said he did not respond to a request for comment.

It says more than 3.2 million students now participate in a system that includes traditional public schools, public charter schools and private school opt
articles.content:
An opinion article published by Florida Politics argues that a lawsuit challenging Florida's school choice system could significantly reduce education options for families across the state.

The article, written by attorney Charles Caulkins, says Florida has expanded education choice since 1999 through charter schools and scholarship programs. It says more than 3.2 million students now participate in a system that includes traditional public schools, public charter schools and private school options supported by scholarships.

Caulkins cites a 2019 Urban Institute study that found students in Florida's tax credit scholarship program were 6 percentage points more likely to enroll in college than comparable students, and says another study of children born in Florida between 1992 and 2002 found stronger academic outcomes in areas with greater competitive pressure. He argues that the lawsuit filed by the Florida Education Association and parents could affect nearly 1 million students who use education alternatives.

The article reflects the author's view that competition in education has improved outcomes and parental choice. It identifies Caulkins as a board member of the Foundation for Florida's Future and the Florida Chamber of Commerce.
